perturbation.original_text: 4. The Receiving Party agrees that: (i) the Information shall be used solely for the purpose described in the preamble to this Agreement; (ii) it will not use any Information disclosed hereunder for any other purpose; and (iii) it will not distribute, disclosure or disseminate Information to anyone except its employees and agents with a need to know and who, in 1 <PAGE> 2 each case, have been informed of the confidential nature of the Information and have agreed to be bound by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Receiving Party agrees that: (i) the data shall be used solely for the purpose described in the preamble to this Agreement; (ii) it will not use any Information disclosed hereunder for any other purpose; and (iii) it will not distribute, disclosure or disseminate Information to anyone except its employees and agents with a need to know and who, in 1 <PAGE> 2 each case, have been informed of the confidential nature of the Information and have agreed to be bound by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Information' with 'data' in clause (i) creates a contradiction within Section 4. The Receiving Party must use 'data' solely for the described purpose, but the following clauses refer to 'Information.' This raises questions about whether restrictions on use, disclosure, and dissemination apply only to 'Information,' or whether 'data' is subject to different (or no) restrictions. This affects enforceability, because using 'communications' from clause (i) to (iii) is now not a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. The Receiving Party agrees that: (i) the data shall be used solely for the purpose described in the preamble to this Agreement; (ii) it will not use any Information disclosed hereunder for any other purpose; and (iii) it will not distribute, disclosure or disseminate Information to anyone except its employees and agents with a need to know and who, in 1 <PAGE> 2 each case, have been informed of the confidential nature of the Information and have agreed to be bound by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Information' with 'data' in clause (i) of Section 4. Clauses (ii) and (iii) still refer to 'Information.' The contradiction arises because the use of 'data' is restricted to the purpose described in the preamble, while the restrictions on use, disclosure, and dissemination apply to 'Information.' This creates a direct conflict and ambiguity, because now 'data' and 'information' are treated differently in the same section, making the terms incompatible and raising questions about which restrictions apply to which term. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as outlined in the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The Information shall be treated as confidential and safeguarded hereunder by the Receiving Party for a period of two (2) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The data shall be treated as confidential and safeguarded hereunder by the Receiving Party for a period of two (2) years.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Information" to "data" within section 5 creates an inconsistency with the rest of the document. Now clause 5 applies only to the data and not to the information as defined in section 1, and later reffered on all other clauses. This means that if the Receiving Party disclosed oral information there will be no breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. Each Party agrees that it will not disclose the subject matter or terms of this Agreement or the discussion between the Parties without the written consent of the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Information" to "data" in section 5 creates a contradiction with section 11. Section 11 refers to the subject matter of the agreement, discussions between the parties, and terms, which could include information that is not necessarily 'data' as narrowly defined. The change restricts the confidentiality obligation in section 5 to only 'data', while section 11 has broader terms, thus causing a direct conflict in what is protected under confidentiality, making the two sections incompatible.

perturbation.original_text: 10. At the Disclosing Party's request, all Information of the Disclosing Party in tangible form, or any copies thereof, that is in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. At the Disclosing Party's request, all data of the Disclosing Party in tangible form, or any copies thereof, that is in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Information' to 'data' in Section 10, the obligation to return or destroy tangible copies of disclosed materials now only applies to 'data'. Section 3 defines 'Information' broadly, encompassing verbal information. This change implies the Receiving Party is no longer obligated to return or destroy tangible records of communications. This contradicts the overall intent to protect all disclosed information, as defined originally, creating uncertainty about the full scope of the return/destruction requirement and is against section 3 which says the party needs to protect the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Information of the Disclosing Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party agrees to protect the Information of the Disclosing Party against unauthorized disclosure and warrants that it applies reasonable safeguards against the unauthorized disclosure Information.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Information' to 'data' in Section 10 narrows the scope of what must be returned or destroyed. Section 3 defines 'Information' broadly, including verbal information. This directly conflicts with Section 3's requirement to protect all 'Information,' because the Receiving Party is now not obligated to return or destroy tangible records of verbal communications. This creates a clear and direct conflict regarding the scope of protection and return obligations, leading to an in-text contradiction under the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement shall not apply to Information that: (a) is in or enters the public domain, through no fault of the Receiving Party; or (b) is or has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party or to a third party without restriction; or (c) is already in the possession of the Receiving Party, without restriction and prior to disclosure of the information hereunder; or (d) is or has been lawfully disclosed by a third party to the Receiving Party without an obligation of confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Agreement shall not apply to data that: (a) is in or enters the public domain, through no fault of the Receiving Party; or (b) is or has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party or to a third party without restriction; or (c) is already in the possession of the Receiving Party, without restriction and prior to disclosure of the information hereunder; or (d) is or has been lawfully disclosed by a third party to the Receiving Party without an obligation of confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: By changing “Information” to “data” in section 6, the exceptions to the confidentiality obligations are changed only to data. The exceptions doesn’t apply to “communications” as defined in section 1 (original), making data not protected under the agreement. Now clause 5 applies only to the data and is not protected, but section 5 says that it needs to be treated as confidential. This creates and in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The data shall be treated as confidential and safeguarded hereunder by the Receiving Party for a period of two (2) years.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Information" to "data" in section 6 narrows the exceptions to confidentiality obligations. Now, only 'data' that falls under the exceptions in section 6 is not treated as confidential. Since section 5 states that 'data' shall be treated as confidential, it creates a contradiction because section 6 now only removes confidentiality obligations from the definition of data and not Information. The change makes sections 5 and 6 incompatible regarding the confidentiality of 'data'.
